title: Reentrant behavior and possible $2/3$ magnetization plateau on the double-trillium langbeinite K$_2$Ni$_2$(SO$_4$)$_3$
authors: Matías G. Gonzalez, Yurii Skourski, Johannes Reuther, Ivica Živković
abstract: K$_2$Ni$_2$(SO$_4$)$_3$ is a member of the langbeinite family, consisting of two intertwined $S=1$ trillium lattices, out of which one is strongly coupled (strong-TL) and the other is weakly coupled (weak-TL). Further inter-trillium interactions give rise to a highly-frustrated Heisenberg Hamiltonian. Despite ordering at low temperatures, K$_2$Ni$_2$(SO$_4$)$_3$ lies close in parameter space to a spin-liquid region that surrounds the tetratrillium limit, where each triangle belonging to strong-TL turns into a tetrahedron by connecting to a single spin from weak-TL. Here, we compare the experimentally determined magnetization process using pulsed magnetic fields up to $40$ T with classical Monte Carlo calculations, uncovering a series of phase transitions at both low and intermediate fields. Furthermore, we reveal a signature of a $2/3$ magnetization plateau consisting of a $1/3$ phase on strong-TL and a fully polarized phase on weak-TL. Although in the classical limit no plateau is expected, we find a very prominent dome structure reflecting the tendency of the system to stabilize this particular spin configuration. The presence of a dome leads to a reentrant phenomenon in which the system recovers the Hamiltonian symmetries when increasing the magnetic field. Finally, we show that this plateau-like phase is also present in the classical Heisenberg model on the single trillium and tetratrillium lattices, indicating its possible presence in the large family of double-trillium langbeinite compounds. Our findings motivate future studies on the presence of the plateau phase in the quantum limit of both trillium and double-trillium materials within the langbeinite family.
summary: This paper explores the magnetic properties of a complex 3D frustrated lattice, identifying a potential 2/3 magnetization plateau and reentrant phase behavior. By combining high-field experiments with classical simulations, the authors show that the observed phase is a characteristic feature of the trillium lattice geometry. This provides a roadmap for searching for quantum plateau states in related langbeinite compounds.
main_problem: Investigating the complex magnetic phase transitions, magnetization process, and the potential existence of a 2/3 magnetization plateau in the double-trillium langbeinite K2Ni2(SO4)3.
main_result: The study identifies a series of phase transitions and a 'dome' structure in the phase diagram that suggests a 2/3 magnetization plateau, characterized by an up-up-down configuration on the strong-TL sublattice and a fully polarized weak-TL sublattice.
method: High-field pulsed magnetic field measurements (up to 40 T) are combined with classical Monte Carlo simulations using 3-component classical vectors.
model_or_system: The system is K2Ni2(SO4)3, a member of the langbeinite family consisting of two intertwined S=1 trillium lattices (strong-TL and weak-TL) described by a highly frustrated 3D Heisenberg Hamiltonian with five exchange interactions.
key_observables: Magnetization (M), magnetization derivative (dM/dH), specific heat (cv), energy per site, and spin structure factors (Szz and Sxy).
important_parameters: Exchange parameters J1 through J5, magnetic field (up to 40 T), and temperature (down to 1.1 K).
main_assumptions: The classical Monte Carlo approach assumes that quantum effects are suppressed by the 3D dimensionality and the spin magnitude (S=1).
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the evolution of longitudinal and transverse spin structure factors, showing the appearance of new Bragg peaks in the up-up-down phase and the reduction of background fluctuations.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the langbeinite system, describes the experimental setup and Hamiltonian, presents magnetization and derivative data, details the Monte Carlo simulation results (phase diagrams and spin configurations), and concludes with the universality of the observed plateau-like behavior.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) The two trillium lattices: strong-TL strongly cou- pled by J5 (blue) and weak-TL weakly coupled by J3 (yellow). (b) The full double-trillium lattice and exchange interactions for K2Ni2(SO4)3 as derived in Ref. [8].

FIG. 2. (a) Pulsed magnetic field measurement of the magnetization and (b) its derivative as a function of the magnetic field B for the K2Ni2(SO4)3 langbeinite. (c,d) A zoom-in into the regions enclosed by the dashed black squares in panel (b). The arrows indicate three possible transitions in the intermediate field region. Panels (e-h) show the same quantities for the cMC calculations at three different temperatures (green, blue, and purple), along with the experimental measurement (red). Note that the cMC temperatures have to be multiplied by S(S + 1) = 2 to be comparable with the experimental temperatures.

FIG. 4. The cMC magnetization of the weak-TL and strong- TL sublattices (colored dashed and full lines, respectively), divided by the corresponding saturated value. The dashed black line indicates the value M/M sub sat = 1/3.
FIG. 3. Specific heat cv as a function of temperature T/J4 and magnetic field h/J4 for four different system sizes L = 3, 6, 9, and 12 (from top to bottom).

FIG. 5. Histograms of the Sz distribution of spins in the J5 trillium sublattice as a function of the temperature for a magnetic field crossing the dome, h = 4 J4. The highest temperature is displayed in red, and the lowest one in blue.
